slides.title: The Mineral Revolution in South Africa
slides.lead: How diamonds at Kimberley and gold on the Witwatersrand changed South Africa forever - and who paid the price.

media.alt: A diamond crystal and a gold bar side by side above a mine headgear tower on brown earth.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='320' height='52' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M96 44 l22 0 12 20 -45 34 -45 -34 12 -20 z' fill='#8fbfe0' stroke='#2f6690' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M96 44 l-22 0 -22 20 44 34 z' fill='#b7dcf2' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='192' y='70' width='72' height='30' rx='4' fill='#e8a13a' stroke='#b6791f' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M192 70 l10 -12 72 0 -10 12 z' fill='#f2c46a'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='4' fill='none'><path d='M150 128 l0 -34 26 34'/><path d='M176 94 l-26 0'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what they already own or use that contains gold or diamonds. Explain that this unit tells how the discovery of these minerals turned South Africa from a mostly farming society into an industrial, mining economy - and how that change was built on cheap, controlled black labour. Preview the two threads: (1) the mineral rushes at Kimberley and the Witwatersrand, and (2) the migrant labour system and social upheaval they created.

slides.title: South Africa before the minerals
slides.lead: In the mid-1800s most people lived from the land, not from mines or factories.

blocks.text: Before 1867, southern Africa had **no big industries**. Most people - African farmers, Boer families and British settlers - lived by farming and herding cattle. There were four main political areas: the British **Cape** and **Natal** colonies, and the Boer republics of the **Transvaal** and the **Orange Free State**.

items: African societies still controlled much of their own land and labour.
There were few towns, few railways and little money in circulation.
The discovery of minerals would change all of this in a single generation.

slides.notes: Stress continuity and change. Set the baseline so learners can measure how dramatic the Mineral Revolution was. Point out that African chiefdoms were still largely self-sufficient - people did not yet depend on wages. Link back to Term 1 (the Industrial Revolution in Britain), because British demand for raw materials and investment is part of why mining grew so fast here.

slides.title: Diamonds are discovered
slides.lead: A shiny pebble picked up near the Orange River set off the first great mineral rush.

blocks.text: In **1867** a young boy, Erasmus Jacobs, found a diamond on a farm near **Hopetown** on the Orange River. Two years later a much larger diamond, later called the **Star of South Africa**, proved the area was rich in diamonds. Thousands of fortune-seekers rushed in.

items: The richest diggings were around what became the town of **Kimberley**.
People came from across the world and across southern Africa to dig.
Diamonds were South Africa's first mineral 'rush' - the start of the revolution.

media.alt: A hand holding up a bright diamond that catches the light beside a winding river.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M20 140 q40 -30 90 -14 q50 16 90 -6' fill='none' stroke='#7fa8c9' stroke-width='8' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M120 40 l20 0 11 18 -41 30 -41 -30 11 -18 z' fill='#8fbfe0' stroke='#2f6690' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M120 40 l-20 0 -20 18 40 30 z' fill='#c7e6f8' opacity='0.8'/><path d='M99 58 l42 0 -21 30 z' fill='#5f9fce'/><g stroke='#e8a13a' stroke-width='3' stroke-linecap='round'><path d='M120 20 l0 -12'/><path d='M150 30 l9 -9'/><path d='M90 30 l-9 -9'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Keep the two famous stones simple: the 1867 find near Hopetown, and the 1869 Star of South Africa that triggered the rush. [SME] Verify the exact naming and dating of the first stones and the Star of South Africa before production - popular accounts vary in detail. The key point for learners is that a chance find launched large-scale, organised mining.

slides.title: The Big Hole
slides.lead: Thousands of diggers turned a hill into the deepest hand-dug hole in the world.

blocks.text: At Kimberley, miners dug straight down into the diamond-bearing rock. Each digger worked a small **claim**, and as the claims went deeper the whole area became one enormous pit - the **Big Hole**. It grew hard and dangerous to work by hand, and only companies with a lot of money could keep mining.

items: Kimberley quickly became one of the largest towns in the country.
Deeper mining needed machines, money and many workers.
Small diggers were slowly bought out by big companies.

media.alt: A cross-section of a deep circular open-pit mine with ramps and tiny workers going down.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='40' width='220' height='120' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M20 40 L110 150 L200 40 Z' fill='#8a6a44'/><path d='M40 40 L110 130 L180 40 Z' fill='#a07d50'/><path d='M64 40 L110 108 L156 40 Z' fill='#b7935f'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6'><path d='M28 52 L192 52'/><path d='M44 70 L176 70'/><path d='M62 90 L158 90'/></g><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='70' cy='60' r='3'/><circle cx='150' cy='62' r='3'/><circle cx='100' cy='84' r='3'/><circle cx='128' cy='96' r='3'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Use the Big Hole to make scale concrete - it is a landmark learners may have heard of or visited. The teaching point is economic: hand-digging by individuals could not continue as mines got deeper, so mining shifted to well-funded companies and wage workers. This sets up the next slide on monopoly and Rhodes.

slides.title: Rhodes, Barnato and De Beers
slides.lead: By 1888 one company controlled almost all diamond mining at Kimberley.

blocks.text: **Cecil John Rhodes** and his rival **Barney Barnato** each bought up claims until, in **1888**, they joined to form **De Beers Consolidated Mines**. This gave De Beers a near-total **monopoly** - control over most of South Africa's diamond production. Rhodes used his wealth to gain huge political power in the Cape.

items: A **monopoly** means one company controls a whole industry.
Controlling supply let De Beers keep diamond prices high.
Rhodes became Prime Minister of the Cape Colony in 1890.

media.alt: A set of scales weighing a bag of money against a diamond, both held by one large hand.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='106' y='34' width='8' height='96' fill='#5c4326'/><rect x='70' y='30' width='80' height='8' rx='4' fill='#5c4326'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='2'><path d='M74 34 L60 66'/><path d='M74 34 L88 66'/><path d='M146 34 L132 66'/><path d='M146 34 L160 66'/></g><path d='M58 66 a16 10 0 0 0 32 0 z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M130 66 a16 10 0 0 0 32 0 z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><circle cx='74' cy='60' r='9' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M146 46 l7 0 4 8 -11 10 -11 -10 4 -8 z' fill='#8fbfe0' stroke='#2f6690' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='96' y='128' width='28' height='10' fill='#5c4326'/></svg>

slides.notes: Introduce the word monopoly clearly - it is a key CAPS concept for this topic. Explain why De Beers wanted to control supply: fewer diamonds on the market keeps prices high. Also flag the link between economic and political power: Rhodes turned mining wealth into political control, which matters later for land and labour laws. Note Rhodes' expansionist ambitions without over-detailing.

slides.title: Gold on the Witwatersrand
slides.lead: In 1886 miners found the world's richest goldfield on a ridge in the Transvaal.

blocks.text: In **1886** gold was found in the **Main Reef** of the **Witwatersrand** (the 'Ridge of White Waters') in the Boer republic of the Transvaal. Almost overnight the mining town of **Johannesburg** sprang up. This goldfield became the largest in the world and pulled money, people and railways towards it.

items: Gold made the poor Transvaal republic suddenly wealthy and important.
Johannesburg grew from open veld into a busy city in just a few years.
Britain now had a strong reason to want control of the Transvaal.

media.alt: A rising city skyline of headgear towers and buildings on a golden ridge under a bright sun.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='200' cy='40' r='22' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#d9b74a'/><g fill='#5c4326'><rect x='30' y='84' width='16' height='36'/><rect x='60' y='72' width='16' height='48'/><rect x='150' y='90' width='16' height='30'/></g><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='3' fill='none'><path d='M96 120 l0 -46 22 46'/><path d='M118 74 l-22 0'/><path d='M118 120 l0 -34 18 34'/></g><rect x='0' y='116' width='240' height='4' fill='#b6791f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Give the meaning of Witwatersrand ('ridge of white waters') and the date 1886. [SME] Popular history credits George Harrison with the 1886 discovery on the farm Langlaagte; confirm the attribution and the exact farm name before production, as sources differ. The big-picture teaching point is that gold shifted the centre of the economy to the Transvaal and set South Africa on the road to conflict between Britain and the Boer republics.

slides.title: Why gold needed big money
slides.lead: Witwatersrand gold lay deep and thin, so mining it took huge investment.

blocks.heading: Deep
blocks.text: The gold ran far underground, so shafts had to be sunk hundreds of metres down.

blocks.heading: Expensive
blocks.text: Machines, explosives and processing needed money that only rich companies had.

blocks.heading: Controlled
blocks.text: A few mining houses and 'Randlords' came to control the whole goldfield.

slides.notes: Explain that unlike alluvial diamonds you could sometimes pick up, Witwatersrand gold was locked in rock deep underground and in low concentrations. This meant individuals could not mine it - it required capital, engineering and cheap labour on a massive scale. Introduce the term 'Randlords' for the wealthy mine owners. This slide is the bridge to the labour question: to make deep-level gold pay, owners needed to keep wages low.

slides.eyebrow: Where did the workers come from?
slides.title: The migrant labour system
slides.lead: The mines were run on the labour of black men who travelled far from home to work.

blocks.text: Mines needed thousands of workers who would accept **low wages**. Owners recruited black men from all over southern Africa on short **contracts**. These **migrant labourers** left their families, worked in the mines for months, and then returned home. Their families stayed behind in the rural areas.

items: Workers were paid far less than white miners for harder, more dangerous jobs.
Because they came and went, owners did not have to pay for family housing.
The system split families and drained the countryside of young men.

media.alt: Figures walking a long path from rural huts towards a distant mine headgear tower.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M10 150 q80 -30 220 -40' fill='none' stroke='#a07d50' stroke-width='10' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><path d='M18 118 a14 10 0 0 1 28 0 z'/><path d='M50 120 a11 8 0 0 1 22 0 z'/></g><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='3' fill='none'><path d='M198 118 l0 -34 18 34'/><path d='M216 84 l-18 0'/></g><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='96' cy='104' r='5'/><rect x='92' y='110' width='8' height='16'/><circle cx='128' cy='96' r='5'/><rect x='124' y='102' width='8' height='16'/><circle cx='158' cy='90' r='5'/><rect x='154' y='96' width='8' height='16'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This is the social heart of the topic - handle it seriously and with empathy. Make clear the deliberate logic: migrant labour kept wages low and let owners avoid paying to house workers' families. Emphasise the human cost - long separations, families split, and a countryside left short of working-age men. Connect to the reserves system that fed workers to the mines.

slides.title: The compound system
slides.lead: Migrant workers were housed in closed, all-male compounds under tight control.

blocks.text: At Kimberley and later on the gold mines, black workers were housed in **compounds** (also called hostels) - fenced barracks where many men lived crowded together, away from their families. At the diamond mines the compounds were **closed**, partly to stop workers hiding diamonds. Life inside was strictly controlled.

items: Workers were separated from families and from the wider town.
Conditions were crowded, and the work underground was dangerous.
The compound system made it easy for owners to control the workforce.

media.alt: Rows of identical barracks buildings inside a tall fence with a single gate.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='240' height='32' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='16' y='40' width='208' height='100' fill='none' stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='4'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='2'><path d='M40 40 L40 140'/><path d='M80 40 L80 140'/><path d='M160 40 L160 140'/><path d='M200 40 L200 140'/></g><rect x='108' y='108' width='24' height='32' fill='#7a5a3a'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='48' y='84' width='40' height='24'/><rect x='100' y='72' width='40' height='24'/><rect x='152' y='84' width='40' height='24'/></g><g fill='#c8b89a'><rect x='56' y='90' width='10' height='10'/><rect x='108' y='78' width='10' height='10'/><rect x='160' y='90' width='10' height='10'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the two reasons compounds mattered to owners: control of the workforce and, at the diamond mines, preventing diamond theft. Keep the tone factual but do not soften the hardship - crowded barracks, family separation and dangerous underground work. Note that the compound and hostel system shaped South African cities and mining for a century afterwards. Avoid gratuitous detail; keep it age-appropriate.

slides.title: Taxes, land and pass laws
slides.lead: Governments used laws to push African people off the land and into the mines.

blocks.text: To make sure the mines had enough cheap workers, governments used the law. **Taxes** (such as hut and poll taxes) had to be paid in money, forcing African men to earn wages. **Pass laws** controlled where black people could travel and live, and land losses meant fewer people could survive by farming alone.

items: Rhodes's **Glen Grey Act (1894)** used a labour tax to push men into work.
Pass laws made it a crime to move without the right papers.
Later the **1913 Natives Land Act** took most farmland from African people.

media.alt: A pass document, a coin stamped with tax, and a fenced-off piece of land, linked by arrows.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='20' y='46' width='50' height='64' rx='3' fill='#dcd3c2' stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='2'/><g fill='#9aa77f'><rect x='28' y='56' width='18' height='18'/></g><g fill='#b7a98d'><rect x='50' y='58' width='14' height='4'/><rect x='28' y='82' width='36' height='4'/><rect x='28' y='92' width='36' height='4'/></g><circle cx='120' cy='78' r='22' fill='#e8a13a' stroke='#b6791f' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M112 78 l16 0 M120 70 l0 16' stroke='#b6791f' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='170' y='58' width='54' height='52' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='2'><path d='M170 58 L170 110 M182 58 L182 110 M196 58 L196 110 M210 58 L210 110 M224 58 L224 110'/><path d='M170 70 L224 70 M170 96 L224 96'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide shows the machinery of labour control - a core CAPS idea: the Mineral Revolution did not run on willing workers alone, but on laws that gave people little choice. Explain each tool simply: money taxes forced wage work, pass laws controlled movement, land loss removed the option of self-sufficient farming. The 1913 Land Act comes after this period but shows where the pattern led; flag it as a bridge to later topics.

slides.title: The Mineral Revolution, 1867-1913

blocks.alt: A timeline of the Mineral Revolution from the 1867 diamond find to the 1913 Land Act.

events.label: Diamond rush and diggings at Kimberley
Gold found on the Witwatersrand; Johannesburg founded
Glen Grey Act pushes Africans into wage labour
South African (Anglo-Boer) War over control of the mines
Natives Land Act deepens land dispossession

slides.notes: Use this to consolidate the sequence before drawing conclusions. Ask learners to notice the two rushes (diamonds then gold) and how quickly control passed to big companies. Point to the 1899-1902 war as partly a struggle over who would control the goldfields, and to 1913 as the moment land loss became national law. [SME] Confirm the Kimberley diggings date (commonly given as 1871) fits the textbook set being used.

slides.title: A country turned upside down
slides.lead: In one generation, mining changed where people lived, how they worked and who held power.

blocks.text: The Mineral Revolution made South Africa an **industrial country**. Cities, railways and banks grew quickly, and huge wealth flowed to mine owners and to Britain. But that wealth was built on **cheap, controlled black labour**, deep racial divisions and the break-up of rural family life. These divisions shaped South Africa long into the future.

items: Railways, towns and banks spread across the country.
African societies lost land, cattle and control over their own labour.
Rivalry over the goldfields helped cause the South African War (1899-1902).

media.alt: A balance scale tipping heavily, with a stack of gold coins on one side and small figures on the other.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='116' y='28' width='8' height='104' fill='#5c4326'/><rect x='96' y='128' width='48' height='10' fill='#5c4326'/><g transform='rotate(-12 120 34)'><rect x='58' y='30' width='124' height='8' rx='4' fill='#5c4326'/><g stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='2'><path d='M64 34 L52 66'/><path d='M64 34 L76 66'/><path d='M176 34 L164 66'/><path d='M176 34 L188 66'/></g><path d='M48 66 a16 10 0 0 0 32 0 z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M160 66 a16 10 0 0 0 32 0 z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g fill='#e8a13a'><rect x='52' y='52' width='24' height='4'/><rect x='54' y='47' width='20' height='4'/><rect x='56' y='42' width='16' height='4'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><circle cx='170' cy='62' r='3'/><circle cx='180' cy='62' r='3'/><circle cx='175' cy='56' r='3'/></g></g></svg>

slides.notes: Draw the big conclusion: the Mineral Revolution modernised the economy but did so unequally, entrenching a racial order in which black South Africans supplied the labour and lost the land while owners and Britain gained the wealth. Connect forward to the South African War (rivalry over gold) and to the later apartheid system, which built on these labour and land patterns. Keep the analysis fair and evidence-based.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: The Mineral Revolution made modern South Africa - and set the pattern of inequality that followed.

blocks.text: Diamonds at **Kimberley** and gold on the **Witwatersrand** turned a farming society into an industrial one within a single generation. Mining created great wealth, cities and railways, but it depended on a **migrant labour** system, the **compound** system and laws that forced African people into low-paid work.

blocks.question: Explain why the discovery of gold on the Witwatersrand was so important for South Africa.
What was the migrant labour system, and how did it affect African families and rural areas?
Describe two ways governments used laws (such as taxes or pass laws) to force people to work on the mines.

slides.notes: Close the loop between the two threads: the mineral rushes and the labour system that made them profitable. Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion; the mark allocations mirror CAPS source-based and extended-writing styles. Remind learners this topic sets up later units - the South African War, segregation and eventually apartheid all build on the land and labour patterns created here.
